desc: Hides or shows the back side of elements when rotated away from the viewer in 3D space.
example:
backface-visibility: hidden;
transform: rotateY(180deg);
whatItDoes: Sets whether the rear face of an element is visible to the user. Turning this to `hidden` is required for flip animations so the front-facing card contents are masked when reversed.
useCase: Essential for double-sided interactive cards, turning pages, rotatable 3D elements, and clean perspective spins.

shortcuts.vscode: Type `backface-visibility` to view autocomplete proposals; select `hidden`.
shortcuts.notepadpp: Write `backface-visibility: hidden;` manually in your file.

desc: Defines the depth perspective scale of a parent container, determining the 3D depth of transformed children.
example:
perspective: 800px;
perspective-origin: center;
whatItDoes: Sets the distance between the viewer and the Z=0 plane to produce realistic perspective depth. Lower values (e.g. 300px) make the 3D skew severe; higher values (1200px) keep it subtle.
useCase: Must be defined on parent wrapper nodes to activate 3D rotations, tilts, and translate-z actions for nested items.

shortcuts.notepadpp: Write `perspective: 800px;` manually inside the target selector.

desc: Aligns flex items along the cross axis of the current line of the flex container.
example: align-items: center; /* Try changing to: stretch, flex-start, flex-end */
whatItDoes:
Defines the default alignment for all flex items along the cross axis (vertical alignment in rows, horizontal alignment in columns). Supports five values:

1. **stretch** (default): Stretches items to fill the container's height (or width if vertical).
2. **flex-start**: Aligns items to the cross-start edge of the container.
3. **center**: Centers items along the cross axis (very common for vertical centering).
4. **flex-end**: Aligns items to the cross-end edge of the container.
5. **baseline**: Aligns items such that their text baselines line up.
syntax: align-items: stretch | flex-start | flex-end | center | baseline;
useCase: Indispensable for vertically centering text labels next to multi-size vector icons inside buttons, or creating aligned bento grids where cards stretch to equal heights.

shortcuts.vscode: Type the Emmet abbreviation `ai` in a CSS rule and press Tab/Enter to expand it to `align-items: ;` with the cursor ready for a value, or just start typing `align-items` and accept VS Code's native CSS IntelliSense suggestion.
shortcuts.notepadpp: With the Emmet plugin installed and its Expand Abbreviation key mapped (no default binding), type `ai` and press that key to expand it to `align-items: ;`. Otherwise type the property manually -- Notepad++ has no native CSS property IntelliSense.

desc: Defines the main-axis direction of the flex container, arranging items horizontally or vertically.
example: flex-direction: column; /* Try changing to: row, row-reverse, column-reverse */
whatItDoes:
Controls the primary alignment axis for all nested flex items. It supports four values: 

1. **row** (default): Arranges items horizontally from left to right.
2. **row-reverse**: Arranges items horizontally from right to left.
3. **column**: Stacks items vertically from top to bottom.
4. **column-reverse**: Stacks items vertically from bottom to top.
useCase: Used to change standard desktop row grids into vertical stacked blocks on responsive mobile screens, or to reverse chat histories and message listings dynamically.

desc: Controls whether the flex container forces all items onto a single line or allows wrapping across multiple lines.
example: flex-wrap: wrap; /* Try changing to: nowrap, wrap-reverse */
whatItDoes:
By default, flex containers force all children onto a single line. Changing this property lets elements drop onto a new line if there is not enough horizontal space, preventing clipping. Supports three values:

1. **nowrap** (default): Forces all items onto a single line, shrinking them if necessary.
2. **wrap**: Allows items to wrap onto multiple lines from top to bottom.
3. **wrap-reverse**: Allows items to wrap onto multiple lines from bottom to top.
useCase: Essential for tag clouds, flow-based image galleries, list badges, and highly responsive multi-card grids that need to adapt seamlessly to small screens.

desc: Aligns flex items along the main axis of the current line of the flex container.
example: justify-content: space-between; /* Try changing to: center, flex-end, space-evenly */
whatItDoes:
Distributes extra free space leftover when flex items are either inflexible or have reached their maximum widths. It controls horizontal spacing in rows and vertical spacing in columns. Supports six major values:

1. **flex-start** (default): Aligns items to the start of the container.
2. **center**: Packs items closely together at the center of the line.
3. **flex-end**: Aligns items to the end of the container.
4. **space-between**: Spreads items evenly; the first item is flush at the start, the last item flush at the end.
5. **space-around**: Distributes items evenly with equal space on both sides of each item.
6. **space-evenly**: Distributes items so that the spacing between any two items, and the space to the edges, is exactly identical.
syntax: justify-content: flex-start | flex-end | center | space-between | space-around | space-evenly;
useCase: Perfect for centering headers, split-navigation layouts (e.g., logo on left, navbar links in center, action buttons on right), and fluid social card gutters.

desc: Sets spacing between grid/flex rows and columns.
example: gap: 16px;

desc: Creates an image consisting of a progressive transition between two or more colors radiating from an origin point.
example: background: radial-gradient(circle at center, #ff7e5f 0%, #feb47b 100%);
whatItDoes: Creates a circular or elliptical gradient radiating from a defined focal point. You can position the hotspot using keywords like 'at top right' or 'at 30% 40%'.
syntax: background: radial-gradient([shape | size] [at position], color1, color2, ...);
useCase: Perfect for ambient light halos, vignette borders, light leaks, realistic sun rays, and futuristic cosmic glowing effects.

shortcuts.vscode: Type `radial-gradient` within a background value -- VS Code's IntelliSense will suggest and autocomplete it.
shortcuts.notepadpp: Notepad++ lacks background function suggestions. Write `radial-gradient()` manually inside property values.

desc: Creates a repeating linear gradient pattern that tiles infinitely across the background.
example: background: repeating-linear-gradient(45deg, #1e1e1e, #1e1e1e 10px, #2a2a2a 10px, #2a2a2a 20px);
whatItDoes: Repeats a linear gradient pattern infinitely. The pattern width is determined by the size difference between the last and preceding color stops.
syntax: background: repeating-linear-gradient(angle, color1, color1 width1, color2 width1, color2 width2);
useCase: Perfect for creating caution stripe animations, paper rule textures, zebra patterns, and brushed mesh dividers.

shortcuts.vscode: Type `repeating-linear-gradient` within a background definition; VS Code autocomplete will trigger.
shortcuts.notepadpp: Notepad++ lacks CSS function autocomplete; type `repeating-linear-gradient()` manually.

desc: Creates a repeating radial gradient pattern of concentric rings that tile infinitely.
example: background: repeating-radial-gradient(circle, #121212, #121212 10px, #2a2a2a 10px, #2a2a2a 20px);
whatItDoes: Fills the element's background with concentric circular or elliptical gradient rings repeating infinitely outward from the center point.
syntax: background: repeating-radial-gradient(shape, color1, color1 radius1, color2 radius1, color2 radius2);
useCase: Useful for custom concentric circles, circular ripples, retro vinyl records, target board layouts, and radar visual backdrops.

shortcuts.vscode: Type `repeating-radial-gradient` -- press Tab/Enter to autocomplete using VS Code's background helpers.
shortcuts.notepadpp: Type `repeating-radial-gradient()` manually as Notepad++ has no value assistance.

desc: Indicating success or completion

useCase: HTML entities like this are essential for displaying special characters that can't be typed directly or would otherwise be interpreted as markup.

shortcuts.vscode: Type `&` inside text content -- VS Code's HTML language suggestions will list matching entities (e.g. `✓`); select it and press Enter to insert it.
shortcuts.notepadpp: Notepad++ has no entity autocomplete. Type `✓` out in full, or use Word Completion (Ctrl+Enter) once the entity has already appeared elsewhere in the open document.

shortcut: Install Emmet in Notepad++ (No Installer, No Admin)
desc: A step-by-step way to get Emmet-style abbreviation expansion working in Notepad++ using only ZIP downloads -- no .exe installer and no administrator password required.
example:
notepadpp-portable/
  notepad++.exe
  plugins/
    EmmetNPP/
      EmmetNPP.dll
      _PyV8.pyd
      PyV8.py
      editor.js
      npp_emmet.py
      emmet/
whatItDoes: Walks through downloading a portable (ZIP) build of Notepad++ plus the Emmet plugin's files directly, and placing them in the correct folder structure by hand -- entirely without running an installer or needing admin/local-admin rights on the machine.
useCase: This is exactly what you need on a locked-down work or school computer where you can't run installers or don't have the admin password, but you still want Emmet-style HTML/CSS abbreviation expansion while coding in Notepad++.

shortcuts.vscode: Not applicable -- VS Code ships Emmet built in, so none of this manual plugin installation is needed there.
shortcuts.notepadpp: Follow the step-by-step guide above: download the portable Notepad++ ZIP and the emmet-npp.zip plugin package, extract both, and drop the plugin's DLL and companion files into plugins\EmmetNPP\ inside your portable Notepad++ folder.

guide.title: Download a portable build of Notepad++ (skip this if you already use one)
guide.detail: Go to the official downloads page at https://notepad-plus-plus.org/downloads/ and choose "Portable (zip)" for your architecture (x64 is correct for almost all modern PCs). This is a plain ZIP archive, not an installer -- right-click it and choose "Extract All" to any folder you own (Desktop, Documents, or even a USB stick). No admin rights are needed because you're not writing to Program Files.

guide.title: Download the Emmet plugin files as a plain ZIP
guide.detail: Skip Notepad++'s built-in Plugins Admin -- it installs into the program's own folder and can fail without write access there. Instead, download "emmet-npp.zip" directly from https://sourceforge.net/projects/npp-plugins/files/Emmet/emmet-npp.zip/download (this is the exact file referenced by the community's own official installation guide on the Notepad++ forum). Extract it to your Desktop -- it contains EmmetNPP.dll plus a few companion files.

guide.title: Create the plugin's subfolder and copy the files in
guide.detail: Since Notepad++ v7.6.3, every plugin must live in its own subfolder named exactly after its DLL. Inside your portable Notepad++ folder, open plugins\ and create a new folder called EmmetNPP. Copy EmmetNPP.dll into it, then copy the remaining companion files from the extracted emmet-npp.zip -- _PyV8.pyd, PyV8.py, editor.js, npp_emmet.py, and the emmet folder -- into that same EmmetNPP folder.

guide.title: Launch your portable Notepad++ and confirm the plugin loaded
guide.detail: Double-click notepad++.exe inside your portable folder (not any Start Menu shortcut, which may point at a different, admin-only installed copy). Open the Plugins menu -- you should now see an Emmet entry. If it's missing, double-check the folder name and file placement from step 3 match exactly.

guide.title: Map a keyboard shortcut for Expand Abbreviation
guide.detail: The Emmet plugin ships with no default keybinding for its "Expand Abbreviation" command. Go to Settings > Shortcut Mapper > Plugin Commands tab, find "Expand Abbreviation", and assign a key combination (Ctrl+Alt+Enter is a safe choice that won't conflict with normal typing; some people remap Tab, but that also affects normal indentation).

guideNote: This legacy Emmet plugin bundles an old embedded PyV8 JavaScript engine and hasn't been updated in years, so it may not load on the very newest Notepad++ builds -- make sure you download the ZIP that matches your Notepad++ bit-width (32-bit vs 64-bit). If it refuses to load at all, the same no-installer, drop-a-DLL-in-a-folder approach works for actively maintained snippet/tag-expansion plugins listed in Notepad++'s official Plugin List (for example WebEdit or FingerText2) -- download their ZIP release from GitHub, and repeat steps 3-5 with that plugin's own DLL and folder name instead.

useCase: Used extensively when building modern web apps needing a A* Search.

desc: A production-ready A* Search (Variant 10) for Algorithms.
whatItDoes: Implements the A* Search pattern with unique style 10.
useCase: Used extensively when building modern web apps needing a A* Search.

desc: A smooth fade-in entrance animation that plays when the element first appears.
example:
Hello there 👋

This card faded in smoothly when the page loaded.
whatItDoes: A smooth fade-in entrance animation that plays when the element first appears.
useCase: Use for hero sections, cards, or any content you want to enter gracefully instead of popping in abruptly.

guide.title: 1. Define the @keyframes
guide.detail: In your CSS file (or a `<style>` tag in the `<head>`), create a `@keyframes fadeIn` block. The `from` state is invisible (`opacity: 0`) and slightly pushed down (`translateY(12px)`); the `to` state is fully visible and in place.

guide.title: 2. Create the animation class
guide.detail: Add a `.fade-in` class that applies `animation: fadeIn 0.8s ease-out both;`. The `both` fill mode keeps the element at the final state after the animation ends (and at the initial state before it starts), preventing a flash of visible content.

guide.title: 3. Attach the class to your element
guide.detail:
Add `class="fade-in"` to any element in your HTML — a `
`, `
`, ``, etc. The animation runs automatically once, as soon as the element renders.

guide.title: 4. Tune duration and easing
guide.detail: Shorter durations (0.3–0.5s) feel snappy; longer (0.8–1.2s) feel elegant. `ease-out` starts fast and slows down, which reads naturally for entrances.

guide.title: 5. Stagger multiple elements (optional)
guide.detail: To fade in a list one item at a time, give each item an increasing `animation-delay`, e.g. `.item:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.1s; }`, `.item:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 0.2s; }`.

guideNote: Prefer animating only `opacity` and `transform` — they are GPU-accelerated and will not cause layout jank.

desc: A looping bounce animation that draws the eye to a button, badge, or icon.
example:
⬇️

Scroll down for more
whatItDoes: A looping bounce animation that draws the eye to a button, badge, or icon.
useCase: Use for scroll indicators, notification badges, or call-to-action arrows that need continuous attention.

guide.title: 1. Define the bounce keyframes
guide.detail: Create `@keyframes bounce` with three stops: at `0%` and `100%` the element sits at its normal position (`translateY(0)`), and at `50%` it rises up (`translateY(-14px)`). Because start and end match, the loop is seamless.

guide.title: 2. Apply the animation infinitely
guide.detail: The `.bounce` class uses `animation: bounce 1s ease-in-out infinite;`. `infinite` makes it loop forever, and `ease-in-out` makes it accelerate and decelerate like real physics.

guide.title: 3. Make inline elements animatable
guide.detail: Transforms do not apply to plain inline elements like `<span>`. Add `display: inline-block;` so the transform takes effect.

guide.title: 4. Attach to your target
guide.detail: Add `class="bounce"` to an arrow icon, emoji, or badge. Keep it on ONE element per screen — multiple bouncing elements compete for attention.

guide.title: 5. Respect reduced motion (recommended)
guide.detail: Wrap the animation in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference) { ... }` so users who disable motion in their OS are not shown a perpetual bounce.

guideNote: Continuous animations should be subtle. If it distracts from reading, reduce the distance or slow the duration.

desc: A typewriter effect where text appears character by character with a blinking cursor.
example: Hello, I build things.
whatItDoes: A typewriter effect where text appears character by character with a blinking cursor.
useCase: Use on portfolio hero sections, terminal-style UIs, or landing pages to add personality to a headline.

guide.title: 1. Understand the trick
guide.detail: The text is fully present in the HTML the whole time. We hide it by giving the element `width: 0` with `overflow: hidden` and `white-space: nowrap`, then animate the width open so characters are revealed left to right.

guide.title: 2. Set up the element
guide.detail: Use a monospace font so every character has the same width — this is what makes `steps()` line up exactly one character per step.

guide.title: 3. Animate the width with steps()
guide.detail: `animation: typing 2.5s steps(22) forwards` reveals the text in 22 discrete jumps (one per character). The `ch` unit equals the width of one character, so `width: 22ch` matches a 22-character string. Count YOUR string length and use that number in both `steps()` and `ch`.

guide.title: 4. Add the blinking cursor
guide.detail: The `border-right` acts as the cursor. A second animation, `blink`, toggles the border color to transparent every half cycle using `step-end`, producing a classic terminal blink.

guide.title: 5. Delay and hold
guide.detail: The `0.4s` delay lets the page settle before typing begins, and `forwards` keeps the text fully revealed when done. To type multiple lines, trigger each line with an increasing `animation-delay`.

guideNote: This pure-CSS version only works for a single line of nowrap text. For multi-line or dynamic text, use a small JS loop instead.

desc: Elements slide and fade into view as the user scrolls to them, powered by IntersectionObserver.
example:
Scroll down ⬇
Section one reveals
Section two reveals
whatItDoes: Elements slide and fade into view as the user scrolls to them, powered by IntersectionObserver.
useCase: Use on marketing and portfolio pages so sections animate in as the visitor scrolls, adding polish without a library.

guide.title: 1. Style the hidden state
guide.detail: Every element that should reveal gets the `.reveal` class: invisible (`opacity: 0`) and shifted down (`translateY(30px)`), with a `transition` on both properties.

guide.title: 2. Style the visible state
guide.detail: The `.reveal.visible` rule is the destination: full opacity and no offset. Because a transition is declared, the browser animates smoothly between the two states whenever the class is added.

guide.title: 3. Create an IntersectionObserver
guide.detail: In a `<script>` at the end of `<body>` (or in your JS file), create `new IntersectionObserver(callback, { threshold: 0.2 })`. The threshold means the callback fires when 20% of the element is inside the viewport.

guide.title: 4. Add the class when visible
guide.detail: Inside the callback, loop over `entries`; if `entry.isIntersecting` is true, add the `visible` class to `entry.target` and call `observer.unobserve(entry.target)` so the animation only ever plays once.

guide.title: 5. Observe all reveal elements
guide.detail: Select every `.reveal` element with `querySelectorAll` and call `observer.observe(el)` on each. New sections only need the `reveal` class in HTML — no extra JS.

guide.title: 6. Verify
guide.detail: Open the page, scroll down slowly, and watch each section fade up as it enters the viewport. In DevTools you can watch the `visible` class appear on each element.

guideNote: IntersectionObserver is far more efficient than listening to `scroll` events — the browser does the visibility math for you.

desc: A lightweight, pure-CSS circular loading spinner.
example: Loading…
whatItDoes: A lightweight, pure-CSS circular loading spinner.
useCase: Use while fetching data, submitting forms, or lazy-loading images — anywhere the user has to wait.

guide.title: 1. Draw the ring
guide.detail: A square `<div>` with `border-radius: 50%` becomes a circle. Give all four borders a faint color, then override just `border-top-color` with a strong color — that one bright arc is what appears to spin.

guide.title: 2. Animate the rotation
guide.detail: One keyframe is enough: `@keyframes spin { to { transform: rotate(360deg); } }`. Apply it with `animation: spin 0.8s linear infinite;`. `linear` is important — any easing makes the spin look like it stutters.

guide.title: 3. Make it accessible
guide.detail: Add `role="status"` and `aria-label="Loading"` so screen readers announce the busy state even though the element has no text.

guide.title: 4. Show and hide with JS
guide.detail: Toggle it around async work: `spinner.hidden = false;` before `await fetch(...)`, and `spinner.hidden = true;` in a `finally` block so it always disappears even if the request fails.

guide.title: 5. Resize for context
guide.detail: Scale by changing `width`/`height` and border thickness together — e.g. 16px/2px for inline button spinners, 44px/4px for page-level loading.

guideNote: Pure-CSS spinners keep animating even when the main thread is busy, unlike GIFs that can freeze.

desc: A Material-style ripple that expands from the exact point where the button is clicked.
example:
<style>
.ripple-btn {
  position: relative;
  overflow: hidden;
  padding: 12px 28px;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 8px;
  background: #4f46e5;
  color: #fff;
  font-size: 15px;
  cursor: pointer;
}
.ripple {
  position: absolute;
  border-radius: 50%;
  background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.5);
  transform: scale(0);
  animation: ripple 0.6s ease-out forwards;
  pointer-events: none;
}
@keyframes ripple { to { transform: scale(4); opacity: 0; } }
</style>
<button class="ripple-btn">Click me anywhere</button>
<script>
document.querySelector('.ripple-btn').addEventListener('click', function (e) {
  const rect = this.getBoundingClientRect();
  const size = Math.max(rect.width, rect.height);
  const circle = document.createElement('span');
  circle.className = 'ripple';
  circle.style.width = circle.style.height = size + 'px';
  circle.style.left = (e.clientX - rect.left - size / 2) + 'px';
  circle.style.top = (e.clientY - rect.top - size / 2) + 'px';
  this.appendChild(circle);
  circle.addEventListener('animationend', () => circle.remove());
});
</script>
whatItDoes: A Material-style ripple that expands from the exact point where the button is clicked.
useCase: Use to give tactile, app-like feedback on primary action buttons.

guide.title: 1. Prepare the button
guide.detail: The button needs `position: relative` (so the ripple positions inside it) and `overflow: hidden` (so the expanding circle is clipped to the button shape).

guide.title: 2. Style the ripple element
guide.detail: The `.ripple` span is an absolutely-positioned white circle starting at `transform: scale(0)`. The keyframe animates it to `scale(4)` while fading `opacity` to 0.

guide.title: 3. Listen for clicks
guide.detail: Add a `click` listener on the button. Inside it, `this.getBoundingClientRect()` gives the button position so we can convert the mouse coordinates (`e.clientX/Y`) into coordinates relative to the button.

guide.title: 4. Spawn the circle at the click point
guide.detail: Create a `<span class="ripple">`, size it to the larger of the button width/height, and offset `left`/`top` by half its size so it is centered exactly under the cursor. Append it to the button — the CSS animation starts automatically.

guide.title: 5. Clean up after the animation
guide.detail: Listen for `animationend` on the circle and call `circle.remove()`. Without this, every click leaves an invisible span behind and the DOM slowly fills up.

guideNote: Setting `pointer-events: none` on the ripple prevents it from swallowing a second quick click.

desc: A button that disables itself and shows a spinner while an async action runs, then restores.
whatItDoes: A button that disables itself and shows a spinner while an async action runs, then restores.
useCase: Use on form submits and save buttons to prevent double-submission and show progress.

guide.title: 1. Build the base button
guide.detail: Use `display: inline-flex` with `gap` so an inline spinner and label sit neatly side by side. Style `:disabled` with reduced opacity and `cursor: not-allowed`.

guide.title: 2. Add a tiny CSS spinner
guide.detail: The `.mini-spin` element is the classic border-circle spinner scaled down to 14px so it fits inside the button text line.

guide.title: 3. Enter the loading state
guide.detail: In the click handler, first save `btn.innerHTML` so you can restore it later. Then set `btn.disabled = true` (blocks double clicks AND signals state to assistive tech) and swap the content to the spinner + "Saving…" label.

guide.title: 4. Do the async work
guide.detail: Replace the `setTimeout` promise with your real call, e.g. `await fetch("/api/save", { method: "POST", body: data })`. Wrap it in `try/catch` so a network failure cannot leave the button stuck in loading forever.

guide.title: 5. Confirm, then restore
guide.detail: On success, briefly show "✓ Saved" before restoring the original label and re-enabling. On failure, show an error label and re-enable immediately so the user can retry.

guideNote: Always disable a submit button during the request — double-submits are one of the most common real-world form bugs.

desc: A button that copies text to the clipboard and confirms with a temporary "Copied!" state.
example: npm install my-lib
whatItDoes: A button that copies text to the clipboard and confirms with a temporary "Copied!" state.
useCase: Use next to code snippets, coupon codes, wallet addresses, or share links.

guide.title: 1. Mark the text source
guide.detail: Give the element containing the copyable text an `id` (here `#snippet`) so the script can read `element.textContent` — this always copies the current text even if it changes dynamically.

guide.title: 2. Call the Clipboard API
guide.detail: `navigator.clipboard.writeText(text)` returns a promise, so mark the handler `async` and `await` it. It only works on secure contexts (https:// or localhost) and must be triggered by a user gesture like this click.

guide.title: 3. Confirm visually
guide.detail: Users need feedback that the copy worked. Swap the button label to "Copied!" immediately after the promise resolves.

guide.title: 4. Handle failure gracefully
guide.detail: In the `catch` branch (permissions denied, insecure context), change the label to hint at manual copying instead of failing silently.

guide.title: 5. Reset the label
guide.detail: A `setTimeout` restores the original label after ~2 seconds so the button is ready for reuse.

guideNote: The old `document.execCommand("copy")` API is deprecated — use `navigator.clipboard` in all new code.

desc: An accessible iOS-style toggle switch built on a real checkbox, so keyboard and forms work for free.
example: Off
whatItDoes: An accessible iOS-style toggle switch built on a real checkbox, so keyboard and forms work for free.
useCase: Use for boolean settings: dark mode, notifications on/off, feature flags.

guide.title: 1. Start with a real checkbox
guide.detail: Wrap an `<input type="checkbox">` and a `<span class="slider">` inside a `<label>`. Because the label wraps the input, clicking anywhere on the switch toggles the checkbox — no JS needed for the toggle itself.

guide.title: 2. Hide the checkbox, keep it functional
guide.detail: Use `opacity: 0; width: 0; height: 0;` — NOT `display: none` — so the input stays focusable and screen-reader accessible.

guide.title: 3. Draw the track and knob
guide.detail: The `.slider` span is the rounded track; its `::before` pseudo-element is the white knob. Both get `transition` so state changes animate.

guide.title: 4. React to the checked state in pure CSS
guide.detail: The sibling selector `input:checked + .slider` recolors the track, and `input:checked + .slider::before` slides the knob 24px right. The checkbox is the single source of truth.

guide.title: 5. Keep keyboard focus visible
guide.detail: Because the input is invisible, add `input:focus-visible + .slider { outline: ... }` so Tab users can see where focus is. Space toggles it, exactly like a native checkbox.

guide.title: 6. Read the value in JS
guide.detail: Listen for the `change` event and read `e.target.checked` to run your logic (save the setting, flip the theme, etc.). In a form, the checkbox submits like any other.

guideNote: Building on a native checkbox gives you keyboard support, form submission, and screen-reader semantics for free — never rebuild those by hand with divs.

desc: An email field that validates as the user types, showing inline success/error messages.
whatItDoes: An email field that validates as the user types, showing inline success/error messages.
useCase: Use on signup and checkout forms — catching typos while typing beats a wall of errors on submit.

guide.title: 1. Structure the field
guide.detail: Pair the `<input>` with a `<label for="...">` (accessibility + bigger click target) and an empty message `<div>` below it. `min-height` on the message prevents the layout jumping when text appears.

guide.title: 2. Define visual states in CSS
guide.detail: Three border states: neutral gray, `.valid` green, `.invalid` red, with a `transition` so the color change is smooth.

guide.title: 3. Listen to the input event
guide.detail: The `input` event fires on every keystroke, paste, and autofill — unlike `change`, which only fires on blur. This is what makes the validation feel "live".

guide.title: 4. Validate the value
guide.detail: Trim whitespace, then test against a simple email regex. Empty input resets to neutral rather than showing an error — do not scold users for a field they have not filled yet.

guide.title: 5. Announce results accessibly
guide.detail: The message div has `aria-live="polite"` so screen readers speak validation messages when they change, without interrupting typing.

guide.title: 6. Still validate on submit
guide.detail: Client-side hints improve UX but are trivially bypassed — always re-validate on the server too.

guideNote: For most cases you can also lean on built-in HTML validation (`type="email"`, `required`, `pattern`) and the `:valid` / `:invalid` CSS pseudo-classes.

desc: A password field with an eye button that toggles between hidden and visible text.
whatItDoes: A password field with an eye button that toggles between hidden and visible text.
useCase: Use on login and signup forms — letting users verify what they typed reduces failed logins dramatically.

guide.title: 1. Position the toggle inside the field
guide.detail: Wrap the input and button in a `position: relative` container, then absolutely position the button at the right edge, vertically centered with `top: 50%; transform: translateY(-50%)`. Add right padding to the input so text never runs under the button.

guide.title: 2. Use type="button"
guide.detail: Inside a `<form>`, a bare `<button>` defaults to `type="submit"` — clicking the eye would submit the form! Always set `type="button"` on the toggle.

guide.title: 3. Flip the input type
guide.detail: The whole trick is one line: switch `input.type` between `"password"` (masked dots) and `"text"` (visible). The value is untouched.

guide.title: 4. Sync the icon and label
guide.detail: Swap the emoji/icon and update `aria-label` so screen-reader users hear the correct action ("Show password" vs "Hide password") each time.

guide.title: 5. Return focus to the field
guide.detail: Calling `pw.focus()` after toggling keeps the user in typing flow instead of stranding focus on the button.

guideNote: Keep `autocomplete="current-password"` so password managers still recognize and fill the field.

desc: A textarea with a live character counter that warns as the limit approaches.
example: 0 / 150
whatItDoes: A textarea with a live character counter that warns as the limit approaches.
useCase: Use for tweets, bios, review boxes, SMS composers — anywhere content has a hard length limit.

guide.title: 1. Set maxlength on the textarea
guide.detail: The `maxlength="150"` attribute makes the browser itself refuse extra characters — the JS counter is feedback on top of that hard limit, not the enforcement.

guide.title: 2. Read the limit from the DOM
guide.detail: The script reads `getAttribute("maxlength")` instead of hard-coding 150, so changing the limit in HTML automatically updates the counter logic.

guide.title: 3. Count on every input event
guide.detail: Update `counter.textContent` with `value.length` on each `input` event, which covers typing, pasting, and cutting.

guide.title: 4. Add warning thresholds
guide.detail: At 90% of the limit the counter turns amber (`.warn`), and at 100% red (`.over`). Progressive color cues let users wrap up naturally rather than hitting a wall.

guide.title: 5. Adapt for no hard limit
guide.detail: If you want a soft limit (allow overflow but flag it), drop `maxlength` and instead disable the submit button when `len > max`.

guideNote: Note that emoji and some characters count as 2+ JS string units — for user-facing counts of complex text, `[...value].length` is more accurate.

desc: A search box that filters a list as you type, debounced so filtering only runs after typing pauses.
whatItDoes: A search box that filters a list as you type, debounced so filtering only runs after typing pauses.
useCase: Use for search-as-you-type over lists, tables, or remote APIs without hammering the server on every keystroke.

guide.title: 1. Render the full list first
guide.detail: A `render(items)` helper turns an array into `<li>` elements (with a friendly "No matches" empty state). Call it once on load so the list is visible before any search.

guide.title: 2. Write the debounce helper
guide.detail: `debounce(fn, delay)` returns a wrapped function that resets a timer on every call — `fn` only actually runs after the calls stop for `delay` ms. This generic helper is reusable anywhere.

guide.title: 3. Filter case-insensitively
guide.detail: Lowercase both the query and each item, then use `.includes(q)` for substring matching. `.startsWith(q)` is an alternative for prefix-style search.

guide.title: 4. Wire it to the input event
guide.detail: Attach the debounced handler to `input`. Typing "straw" fires the event 5 times but filters only once, 250ms after the last keystroke.

guide.title: 5. Swap in a real API
guide.detail: For remote search, replace the local filter with `fetch("/api/search?q=" + encodeURIComponent(q))` inside the debounced function — debouncing is what keeps you from sending a request per keystroke.

guideNote: 250–300ms is the sweet spot for search debounce: fast enough to feel live, slow enough to skip intermediate keystrokes.

desc: A complete signup form that validates all fields on submit, focuses the first error, and blocks bad submissions.
whatItDoes: A complete signup form that validates all fields on submit, focuses the first error, and blocks bad submissions.
useCase: Use as the backbone for any signup, checkout, or settings form that needs client-side validation.

guide.title: 1. Disable native bubbles with novalidate
guide.detail: Adding `novalidate` to the `<form>` turns off the browser’s default popup validation so your custom inline messages take over completely.

guide.title: 2. Describe rules as data
guide.detail: Each rule object holds the input id, the error-element id, a `test(value)` function, and a message. Adding a field to the form later = adding one object to the array, no new logic.

guide.title: 3. Intercept the submit event
guide.detail: Listen on the FORM’s `submit` event (not the button’s click — submit also fires when pressing Enter in a field) and call `e.preventDefault()` to stop the page reload while validating.

guide.title: 4. Validate every field, not just the first
guide.detail: Loop over all rules, toggling the `.invalid` class and error text per field, so the user sees ALL problems at once instead of fixing them one submit at a time.

guide.title: 5. Focus the first error
guide.detail: Track the first failing input and call `.focus()` on it — keyboard and screen-reader users land directly on what needs fixing.

guide.title: 6. Submit for real
guide.detail: When every rule passes, send the data with `fetch` and `new FormData(form)`, or remove `preventDefault` conditionally to allow a classic form POST. Always re-validate on the server.

guideNote: Pattern to remember: rules-as-data + single submit handler. It scales from 3 fields to 30 without growing the logic.

desc: An accessible modal with overlay that opens, closes on button, overlay click, or Escape key.
example:
Confirm action

Are you sure you want to continue? This cannot be undone.
whatItDoes: An accessible modal with overlay that opens, closes on button, overlay click, or Escape key.
useCase: Use for confirmations, forms, and alerts that must interrupt the flow and demand a decision.

guide.title: 1. Build the overlay + modal structure
guide.detail: A full-screen fixed `.overlay` (`inset: 0`) darkens the page and centers the `.modal` card with `display: grid; place-items: center`. It starts as `display: none` and the `.open` class shows it.

guide.title: 2. Add dialog semantics
guide.detail: `role="dialog"`, `aria-modal="true"`, and `aria-labelledby` pointing at the title tell assistive tech this is a modal and what it is about.

guide.title: 3. Wire open and close
guide.detail: Two tiny functions toggle the `.open` class. Everything else — the trigger button, Cancel, Confirm — just calls them.

guide.title: 4. Close on overlay click, but not modal click
guide.detail: The listener checks `e.target === overlay` so clicks on the dark backdrop close the modal, but clicks inside the card (which bubble up through it) do not.

guide.title: 5. Close on Escape
guide.detail: A document-level `keydown` listener for `e.key === "Escape"` matches what users expect from every native dialog.

guide.title: 6. Manage focus
guide.detail: On open, move focus into the dialog (here the Cancel button); on close, return focus to the button that opened it. This keeps keyboard users oriented.

guideNote: Modern browsers also ship a native `<dialog>` element with `showModal()` that gives you focus trapping and Escape handling for free — worth using when you do not need to support very old browsers.

desc: Stackable toast notifications that slide in, auto-dismiss after a delay, and can be closed manually.
whatItDoes: Stackable toast notifications that slide in, auto-dismiss after a delay, and can be closed manually.
useCase: Use for non-blocking feedback: saved settings, sent messages, background errors — anywhere an alert() would be too disruptive.

guide.title: 1. Create one fixed container
guide.detail: A single `#toasts` div is fixed to the bottom-right corner with `flex-direction: column; gap`. Every toast is appended into it, so multiple toasts stack automatically.

guide.title: 2. Write a reusable showToast() function
guide.detail: It takes `(message, type, duration)` and builds the toast DOM with `createElement` + `textContent` (never `innerHTML` with user text — that invites XSS).

guide.title: 3. Style variants with a modifier class
guide.detail: The type argument becomes a class (`success` / `error`) that just changes the accent border color. Add `info` or `warning` variants the same way.

guide.title: 4. Animate the entrance
guide.detail: The `slideIn` keyframes start off-screen right (`translateX(110%)`) — since the element is appended already, only a `from` frame is needed; it animates to its natural position.

guide.title: 5. Auto-dismiss with a manual escape hatch
guide.detail: `setTimeout(() => toast.remove(), duration)` clears it automatically, and the ✕ button removes it instantly for impatient users.

guide.title: 6. Announce to screen readers
guide.detail: `aria-live="polite"` on the container makes assistive tech read each toast when it appears, without needing focus.

guideNote: Call `showToast()` from anywhere in your app — after fetch success/failure is the classic use.

desc: A keyboard-friendly tab switcher that shows one panel at a time.
example:
Edit your name, avatar and bio here.
Manage payment methods and invoices.
Change password and enable 2FA.
whatItDoes: A keyboard-friendly tab switcher that shows one panel at a time.
useCase: Use for settings pages, product detail sections, dashboards — dense content grouped into views without navigation.

guide.title: 1. Use ARIA tab semantics
guide.detail: Buttons get `role="tab"` inside a `role="tablist"`; each panel gets `role="tabpanel"`. `aria-controls` links a tab to its panel id, and `aria-labelledby` links back. Screen readers then announce "tab 1 of 3, selected".

guide.title: 2. Hide inactive panels with the hidden attribute
guide.detail: The native `hidden` attribute (plus a `[hidden] { display: none; }` safety rule) removes inactive panels from both view and the accessibility tree — no custom show/hide classes needed.

guide.title: 3. Style the selected state via aria-selected
guide.detail: Instead of a separate `.active` class, style `[aria-selected="true"]` directly. The accessibility state and the visual state can never drift apart.

guide.title: 4. Centralize switching in activate(index)
guide.detail: One function loops over all tabs, sets `aria-selected`, and toggles each linked panel’s `hidden`. Every interaction (click, keyboard) funnels through it.

guide.title: 5. Add arrow-key navigation
guide.detail: Left/Right arrows move between tabs with wrap-around (the `% tabs.length` trick), matching the standard ARIA tabs keyboard pattern.

guide.title: 6. Add more tabs declaratively
guide.detail: To add a fourth tab, add one `<button role="tab">` and one panel with matching ids — the script picks them up automatically since it queries by role.

guideNote: This is the W3C ARIA "tabs" pattern in its simplest form — the same structure scales up to any design system.

desc: An expandable FAQ list built on native <details>/<summary> with smooth styling — zero JavaScript required.
example:
Do I need an account to order?
No — guest checkout is available. Creating an account lets you track orders and save addresses.
What is the return policy?
Returns are free within 30 days of delivery. Items must be unused and in original packaging.
How long does shipping take?
Standard shipping takes 3–5 business days; express options are shown at checkout.
whatItDoes: An expandable FAQ list built on native <details>/<summary> with smooth styling — zero JavaScript required.
useCase: Use for FAQs, settings groups, and long documents — collapsing content keeps pages scannable.

guide.title: 1. Use native details/summary
guide.detail: Each Q&A pair is a `<details>` element whose `<summary>` is the question. The browser handles toggling, keyboard support (Enter/Space), and accessibility natively — no JS at all.

guide.title: 2. Remove the default triangle
guide.detail: `list-style: none` on the summary (plus the `::-webkit-details-marker` rule for Safari) removes the default disclosure triangle so you can add your own indicator.

guide.title: 3. Add a rotating plus icon
guide.detail: A `::after` pseudo-element renders "+". When the panel opens, the `[open]` attribute appears on `<details>`, and the selector `details[open] summary::after` rotates it 45° into an "×".

guide.title: 4. Style the answer body
guide.detail: Wrap the answer in a div for padding control. Everything inside `<details>` other than the summary is hidden until expanded.

guide.title: 5. Optional: one-open-at-a-time
guide.detail: Give every details element the same `name="faq"` attribute (supported in modern browsers) and the browser closes the others automatically, like a classic accordion.

guideNote: Reaching for native elements first (`<details>`, `<dialog>`, `<select>`) means less code, fewer bugs, and built-in accessibility.

desc: A dark/light theme switcher that remembers the choice in localStorage and respects the OS preference.
example: This panel recolors instantly when the theme changes — and the choice survives a page reload.
whatItDoes: A dark/light theme switcher that remembers the choice in localStorage and respects the OS preference.
useCase: Use as the theme system for any site — the CSS variable pattern scales from this demo to a full design system.

guide.title: 1. Define theme colors as CSS variables
guide.detail: Declare defaults on `:root` (light theme), then override the SAME variable names under `[data-theme="dark"]`. Components only ever reference `var(--bg)` etc., so they are theme-agnostic.

guide.title: 2. Switch theme by flipping one attribute
guide.detail: Setting `data-theme="dark"` on the root container swaps every variable at once. In a real site put the attribute on `<html>` (`document.documentElement`), so the entire page recolors.

guide.title: 3. Respect the OS preference initially
guide.detail: On first visit (no saved choice), `matchMedia("(prefers-color-scheme: dark)")` detects the user’s system setting and uses it as the default.

guide.title: 4. Persist the explicit choice
guide.detail: When the user clicks the toggle, store the result in `localStorage`. On future loads, the saved value takes priority over the OS preference.

guide.title: 5. Keep the button label in sync
guide.detail: A single `setTheme()` function updates both the attribute and the button text, so state can never disagree with the UI.

guide.title: 6. Avoid the flash of wrong theme
guide.detail: In production, run the localStorage check in a tiny inline `<script>` in the `<head>` BEFORE the page renders, so users never see a white flash before dark mode applies.

guideNote: Add `transition: background 0.3s, color 0.3s` to themed containers so switching feels smooth rather than jarring.
